searchLedger.hypothesis: Exact 10,245 document count is not a weakness

searchActions.result: The fixture default is 10,245 docs in iterateCorpusSizedDocuments at lib/eval/fixtures/bm25-packed-fixture.ts:165-167, and the gate asserts that same count.

searchLedger.rationale: Pinning corpus size prevents fixture drift from silently shrinking the workload and making the RSS budget easier.
searchLedger.ruledOutReason: The assertion is a useful guard against accidental corpus shrinkage, not a brittle unrelated check.

searchLedger.hypothesis: Mutable-postings size check is adequate for current ownership model

searchActions.result: The test checks packedDirtyTerms.size and packedMutablePostings.size after rebuild. In current code, getPackedPosting stores the same PackedPostingStore in packedPostings and deletes it from packedMutablePostings at lib/search/bm25-index.ts:974-975.

searchLedger.rationale: The typed arrays retained in packedPostings are the live index, not a duplicate mutable copy. This does not prove OS-level memory release, but it does prove the stale mutable map ownership that caused the functional warmup regression is gone.
searchLedger.ruledOutReason: Given the current implementation, cleared mutable ownership plus retained packedPostings is the intended state.

title: RSS gate samples final RSS, not peak warmup RSS
evidence: The test captures beforeRss at line 114, performs all addDocumentFields/finalizePackedPostings work at lines 117-120, then samples process.memoryUsage().rss once at line 123. There is no in-loop max tracking, forced GC, worker reset, or heap isolation in this test.
rationale: The acceptance says peak RSS spike <=150MB, but this can pass if a transient spike occurs and is released/reused before the final sample. It can also pass artificially low when the worker process already has high resident arenas and new allocations reuse those pages. With the claimed 134.3MB vs 150MB margin, this is false-green-prone and potentially flaky.

title: Realistic RSS corpus bypasses the production warmup scheduler
evidence: The RSS test directly iterates fixture docs and calls index.addDocumentFields/finalizePackedPostings. The production warmup path is BM25Index.rebuildFromDatabase, which batches syncChangedRows through timers and finalizes after the last batch in lib/search/bm25-index.ts:760-803. That path is only covered on 600 synthetic docs in lines 146-197, not by the 10,245-doc RSS gate.
rationale: A regression in DB row hydration, batched scheduling, or final-batch finalization at realistic scale would not affect the measured 134MB path. The hard gate therefore does not fully exercise the warmup path it claims to bound.

title: Corpus fixture never exercises width-promotion branches
evidence: Default corpus count is 10,245, so packed numeric doc ids stay <=10,244, below PACKED_POSTING_MAX_NARROW_DOC_ID=65,535. Each body uses a 256-term window over about 6.7KB per doc from the 69.2MB target, so per-term field tf is single digits, below the 255 and 65,535 promotion thresholds. The vocabulary is 4,096 body words plus fixture/title/path tokens, below the 65,535 term-id promotion threshold.
rationale: The new promotion code in bm25-index.ts:925-932 and bm25-index.ts:1074-1114 can be broken while the RSS gate still passes. The ranking parity tests use 600 or 6 docs, so they also do not cover promoted packed arrays.

title: Latency hard gate is a benchmark-style wall-clock assertion
evidence: The same test measures one wall-clock duration around corpus generation/indexing and asserts warmupMs <= 10_000 at lines 122-133.
rationale: This can fail under CI CPU contention or slower host classes without indicating an RSS regression. It is less severe than the RSS issue because it does exercise real work, but it makes the acceptance gate noisier unless run in a controlled perf lane.
